inline_review:
commit 41aa1a67a06a217f41a8143580f1fb35ee27fe60
Author: Aleksandr Nogikh <nogikh@google.com>
x86/kexec: Disable KCOV instrumentation after load_segments()

This commit disables KCOV instrumentation for machine_kexec_64.o and
physaddr.o to prevent crashes during kexec. The crashes occur because
load_segments() invalidates the GS base, which KCOV relies on for per-CPU
data access.

> diff --git a/arch/x86/kernel/Makefile b/arch/x86/kernel/Makefile
> index e9aeeeafad17..41b1333907de 100644
> --- a/arch/x86/kernel/Makefile
> +++ b/arch/x86/kernel/Makefile
[ ... ]
> @@ -43,6 +43,16 @@ KCOV_INSTRUMENT_dumpstack_$(BITS).o			:= n
>  KCOV_INSTRUMENT_unwind_orc.o				:= n
>  KCOV_INSTRUMENT_unwind_frame.o				:= n
>  KCOV_INSTRUMENT_unwind_guess.o				:= n
> +# Disable KCOV to prevent crashes during kexec: load_segments() invalidates
> +# the GS base, which KCOV relies on for per-CPU data.
> +# As KCOV && KEXEC compatibility should be preserved (e.g. syzkaller is
> +# using it to collect crash dumps during kernel fuzzing), we could either
> +# selectively disable KCOV instrumentation, which can be fragile, or add
> +# more checks to KCOV, which would slow it down.
> +# As a compromise solution, let's disable KCOV instrumentation for the
> +# whole file. If its coverage is ever needed, we should consider other
> +# approaches.
> +KCOV_INSTRUMENT_machine_kexec_64.o			:= n

Does this fix cover the CONFIG_KEXEC_JUMP path where execution returns to a
KCOV-instrumented kernel?

During a kexec jump, relocate_kernel returns execution back to machine_kexec()
via the virtual_mapped label without restoring MSR_GS_BASE. The C code then
checks image->preserve_context and calls restore_processor_state() in
arch/x86/power/cpu.c.

Since arch/x86/power/cpu.o is not excluded from KCOV instrumentation, wouldn't
the compiler insert a call to __sanitizer_cov_trace_pc() at the entry of
restore_processor_state(), crashing the kernel when it attempts to access
per-CPU data via the invalid GS base?

> diff --git a/arch/x86/mm/Makefile b/arch/x86/mm/Makefile
> index 5b9908f13dcf..ea3a31b54e49 100644
> --- a/arch/x86/mm/Makefile
> +++ b/arch/x86/mm/Makefile
[ ... ]
> @@ -4,6 +4,16 @@ KCOV_INSTRUMENT_tlb.o			:= n
>  KCOV_INSTRUMENT_mem_encrypt.o		:= n
>  KCOV_INSTRUMENT_mem_encrypt_amd.o	:= n
>  KCOV_INSTRUMENT_pgprot.o		:= n
> +# Disable KCOV to prevent crashes during kexec: load_segments() invalidates
> +# the GS base, which KCOV relies on for per-CPU data.
> +# As KCOV && KEXEC compatibility should be preserved (e.g. syzkaller is
> +# using it to collect crash dumps during kernel fuzzing), we could either
> +# selectively disable KCOV instrumentation, which can be fragile, or add
> +# more checks to KCOV, which would slow it down.
> +# As a compromise solution, let's disable KCOV instrumentation for the
> +# whole file. If its coverage is ever needed, we should consider other
> +# approaches.
> +KCOV_INSTRUMENT_physaddr.o		:= n

Is disabling KCOV for all of physaddr.c an overly broad fix that causes
unnecessary loss of coverage for core memory primitives like __phys_addr()?

The crash in physaddr.c appears to happen because virt_to_phys(control_page)
is evaluated as an argument to relocate_kernel_ptr() after load_segments()
invalidates the GS base. Under CONFIG_DEBUG_VIRTUAL=y, virt_to_phys() expands
to an out-of-line call to __phys_addr().

This seems to violate the explicit comment in the code:
"load_segments() resets GS to 0. Don't make any function call after here".

Could we instead pre-evaluate virt_to_phys(control_page) into a local variable
before calling load_segments()? This would ensure no function calls are made
while the GS base is invalid, eliminating the need to disable KCOV for
physaddr.c entirely.

@technical-patterns.md

Target Commit:
commit 41aa1a67a06a217f41a8143580f1fb35ee27fe60
Author: Aleksandr Nogikh <nogikh@google.com>
Date:   Tue Mar 17 22:03:19 2026 +0000

    x86/kexec: Disable KCOV instrumentation after load_segments()
    
    The load_segments() function changes segment registers, invalidating
    GS base (which KCOV relies on for per-cpu data). When CONFIG_KCOV is
    enabled, any subsequent instrumented C code call (e.g.
    native_gdt_invalidate()) begins crashing the kernel in an endless
    loop.
    
    To reproduce the problem, it's sufficient to do kexec on a
    KCOV-instrumented kernel:
    $ kexec -l /boot/otherKernel
    $ kexec -e
    
    The real-world context for this problem is enabling crash dump
    collection in syzkaller. For this, the tool loads a panic kernel
    before fuzzing and then calls makedumpfile after the panic. This
    workflow requires both CONFIG_KEXEC and CONFIG_KCOV to be enabled
    simultaneously.
    
    Adding safeguards directly to the KCOV fast-path
    (__sanitizer_cov_trace_pc()) is also undesirable as it would
    introduce an extra performance overhead.
    
    Disabling instrumentation for the individual functions would be too
    fragile, so let's fix the bug by disabling KCOV instrumentation for
    the entire machine_kexec_64.c and physaddr.c. If coverage-guided
    fuzzing ever needs these components in the future, we should consider
    other approaches.
    
    The problem is not relevant for 32 bit kernels as CONFIG_KCOV is not
    supported there.
    
    Reviewed-by: Dmitry Vyukov <dvyukov@google.com>
    Signed-off-by: Aleksandr Nogikh <nogikh@google.com>
    Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org

diff --git a/arch/x86/kernel/Makefile b/arch/x86/kernel/Makefile
index e9aeeeafad17..41b1333907de 100644
--- a/arch/x86/kernel/Makefile
+++ b/arch/x86/kernel/Makefile
@@ -43,6 +43,16 @@ KCOV_INSTRUMENT_dumpstack_$(BITS).o			:= n
 KCOV_INSTRUMENT_unwind_orc.o				:= n
 KCOV_INSTRUMENT_unwind_frame.o				:= n
 KCOV_INSTRUMENT_unwind_guess.o				:= n
+# Disable KCOV to prevent crashes during kexec: load_segments() invalidates
+# the GS base, which KCOV relies on for per-CPU data.
+# As KCOV && KEXEC compatibility should be preserved (e.g. syzkaller is
+# using it to collect crash dumps during kernel fuzzing), we could either
+# selectively disable KCOV instrumentation, which can be fragile, or add
+# more checks to KCOV, which would slow it down.
+# As a compromise solution, let's disable KCOV instrumentation for the
+# whole file. If its coverage is ever needed, we should consider other
+# approaches.
+KCOV_INSTRUMENT_machine_kexec_64.o			:= n
 
 CFLAGS_head32.o := -fno-stack-protector
 CFLAGS_head64.o := -fno-stack-protector
diff --git a/arch/x86/mm/Makefile b/arch/x86/mm/Makefile
index 5b9908f13dcf..ea3a31b54e49 100644
--- a/arch/x86/mm/Makefile
+++ b/arch/x86/mm/Makefile
@@ -4,6 +4,16 @@ KCOV_INSTRUMENT_tlb.o			:= n
 KCOV_INSTRUMENT_mem_encrypt.o		:= n
 KCOV_INSTRUMENT_mem_encrypt_amd.o	:= n
 KCOV_INSTRUMENT_pgprot.o		:= n
+# Disable KCOV to prevent crashes during kexec: load_segments() invalidates
+# the GS base, which KCOV relies on for per-CPU data.
+# As KCOV && KEXEC compatibility should be preserved (e.g. syzkaller is
+# using it to collect crash dumps during kernel fuzzing), we could either
+# selectively disable KCOV instrumentation, which can be fragile, or add
+# more checks to KCOV, which would slow it down.
+# As a compromise solution, let's disable KCOV instrumentation for the
+# whole file. If its coverage is ever needed, we should consider other
+# approaches.
+KCOV_INSTRUMENT_physaddr.o		:= n
 
 KASAN_SANITIZE_mem_encrypt.o		:= n
 KASAN_SANITIZE_mem_encrypt_amd.o	:= n

    "thought": "**Analyzing the KCOV Disablement for x86 Kernel Modules**

Okay, so I'm looking at this commit that disables KCOV instrumentation for `arch/x86/kernel/machine_kexec_64.o` and `arch/x86/mm/physaddr.o`. The stated reason is that `load_segments()` in the kexec context invalidates the GS base, which KCOV relies on for per-CPU data. This causes crashes in instrumented code, specifically when functions like `native_gdt_invalidate()` are called. Disabling KCOV for those whole files is the proposed solution.

My immediate thought is, let's trace this. `load_segments()` is the culprit. In the kexec flow, does `machine_kexec_64.c` call it? Ah, no, it defines `machine_kexec()`, which is responsible for setting up segments during the kexec process. So, it looks like `load_segments()` is called there. If `load_segments()` is changing the segment registers and invalidating the GS base, then KCOV will crash when trying to access per-CPU data. That's a classic x86_64 issue with KCOV and segment registers.

The more puzzling part is why `physaddr.o` is also disabled. It has to be that `machine_kexec_64.c` calls functions in `physaddr.c` after this segment register manipulation. Let's dig in. It has to be something related to physical address manipulation, as the name of the file suggests.

Now, is disabling KCOV for the entire file a reasonable approach? Yeah, it's pretty common in this kind of low-level architecture code, especially when it runs in weird states. Early boot, suspend/resume, kexec, all of those are potential problem areas. The architecture might not be fully initialized. Per-CPU variables, the stack, interrupts, all could be in an undefined state. We see this pattern all over `arch/x86/kernel/Makefile` and `arch/x86/mm/Makefile`.

Okay, but there's an architectural concern here. This commit is functionally safe, and it's not changing anything, but why `physaddr.o`? That's bothering me. In x86, `__pa()` and `__va()` are in `physaddr.c`, along with functions such as `phys_addr_valid()` and `__phys_addr()`. So if `machine_kexec_64.c` disables KCOV but *calls* something like `__phys_addr()` from `physaddr.c`, it would cause a KCOV crash. This makes sense. `__phys_addr()` could easily be called after GS is invalidated during kexec. The function likely can't handle being instrumented.

Wait a second. This is an important piece of information. `__phys_addr()` is *heavily* used throughout the kernel! Disabling KCOV for *all* of `physaddr.o` means we lose coverage for `__phys_addr()` *everywhere* it's called! That's a big loss. Is this even a reasonable tradeoff? I think I need to check how frequently `physaddr.o` is actually used.

I'm going to run `default_api:read_files` on `arch/x86/mm/physaddr.c`. Let's get some visibility into where this function is called.

    "thought": "**Analyzing the Kernel's Kexec KCOV Crash**

Okay, so I've been digging into this kexec issue and it seems pretty clear what's happening. The `machine_kexec()` function is the culprit. It disables interrupts, tracing, and then calls `load_segments()`. The crash happens because `load_segments()` changes segment registers, thus invalidating GS, which KCOV relies on.

After `load_segments()`, there's a call to `relocate_kernel_ptr()`. unsigned long __phys_addr(unsigned long x)
```c
{
	unsigned long y = x - __START_KERNEL_map;

	/* use the carry flag to determine if x was < __START_KERNEL_map */
	if (unlikely(x > y)) {
		x = y + phys_base;

		VIRTUAL_BUG_ON(y >= KERNEL_IMAGE_SIZE);
	} else {
		x = y + (__START_KERNEL_map - PAGE_OFFSET);

		/* carry flag will be set if starting x was >= PAGE_OFFSET */
		VIRTUAL_BUG_ON((x > y) || !phys_addr_valid(x));
	}

	return x;
}
```

The critical window is really between `load_segments()` and the end of `relocate_kernel()`. Since `relocate_kernel()` is assembly, it's uninstrumented, thankfully. The only C code that executes there is the evaluation of arguments to `relocate_kernel_ptr()`, specifically `virt_to_phys()`.

So, disabling KCOV for `machine_kexec_64.c` effectively prevents compiler-inserted KCOV callbacks, like those before `relocate_kernel_ptr()` or within `__phys_addr()`, from causing a crash. The author opted to disable KCOV at the *file* level instead of applying `noinstr` to specific functions. The commit message clarifies this: applying `noinstr` directly to individual functions would introduce performance overhead. Disabling it on the file level is less fragile because if a function in the file calls another function, that will also need to be annotated as `noinstr` which creates an ongoing maintenance task. `phys_addr_valid()`, on the other hand, is `static inline` in `arch/x86/mm/physaddr.h`. So it behaves similarly to `virt_to_phys()`.

    "thought": "**KCOV Disable Patch Analysis for kexec**

Okay, let's break this down. My initial thought is to confirm the logic behind the KCOV disable patch for `machine_kexec_64.o` and `physaddr.o`. The core concern is KCOV crashing during kexec due to the invalidation of the GS base by `load_segments()`. This invalidates access to per-CPU data that KCOV relies upon, specifically through `__sanitizer_cov_trace_pc()`, and specifically the access of `current`.

First, I need to understand what's happening. `memory_is_poisoned` is a shadow map check, and if it fails then `kasan_report` is triggered, which in turn *will* access per-cpu data. This means that if KASAN detects a bug, it *should* crash, but that's expected.

KCOV, unlike KASAN, crashes in *normal* operation because the instrumentation is unconditional. The key here is the unconditional call to `__sanitizer_cov_trace_pc()` which reads `current`. Since this is problematic, this is the reason for the original disable.

I checked to see if KASAN was disabled for `machine_kexec_64.o`, but I confirmed it's not. However, KASAN would only crash if it detected a memory error. The relocation code `relocate_kernel_ptr()` stores data, so could potentially be instrumented, but after a code review this is unlikely to cause an issue because the fast path does not call `kasan_report`.

Then, I'm verifying the KCOV disable logic. I reviewed the diff and confirmed the commit message matches the Makefile changes: specifically, disabling KCOV instrumentation for these two object files. This matches my understanding of the problem. Also there are no spelling issues and the commit logic matches the reasoning from the diff.

I then check for corner cases. I confirm that if `CONFIG_KEXEC_CORE` is *not* enabled, then the object file is not built, so the Makefile setting would simply have no effect, which is fine.

I also confirm that `machine_kexec_64.o` is the *only* object file built from `machine_kexec_64.c` that's kexec related. I confirmed that kexec-bzimage64.o, is related to kexec but does not contain the `machine_kexec` path.

Then, I check if any other functions are called after `load_segments()` in the relevant code path. I see that `relocate_kernel_ptr()` stores data into `image->start`, which might get instrumented by KASAN.

I quickly check for other potential issues related to execution flow, loop control, and validation. However, the commit only *changes* the Makefile to *disable* instrumentation. The commit does not include any C code changes.

I'm confident that the lack of code changes means that the tasks are fine.

Then I check for linker errors or LTO symbol loss, but since KCOV disable simply removes instrumentation flags, it should not have those issues. It's a standard pattern used in multiple places within the kernel.

I then check for KCOV disable. I check `physaddr.o` which only has `__phys_addr` and `__virt_addr_valid`, and the disable is fine here.

Finally I check about other SANs. I confirm that KCSAN is disabled in `arch/x86/mm/Makefile`, and that KMSAN is disabled for `kmsan_shadow.o`. I look into potential conflicts with KMSAN or KCSAN, but I see from the global configuration that these should not be an issue.

The conclusion is that the KCOV disable patch is logically sound, and correctly addresses the crash during kexec by preventing KCOV's unconditional access to per-CPU data after `load_segments()` invalidates the GS base. I found no other issues or concerns in this commit.

    "thought": "**Analyzing KCOV Instrumentation in `machine_kexec`**

Okay, so I'm looking at this `machine_kexec` function, specifically how it interacts with KCOV instrumentation. The key issue is this: `load_segments()` resets the `GS` base, and KCOV, as implemented in `__sanitizer_cov_trace_pc()`, relies on per-CPU data accessed through `GS`. That means, after `load_segments()`, any KCOV instrumentation that touches per-CPU variables will crash.

Initially, I thought the fix, disabling KCOV in `physaddr.c`, was sufficient, but that's a coverage loss. Let's see... the `relocate_kernel_ptr()` call is where the problem lies. The argument `virt_to_phys(control_page)` is evaluated *after* `load_segments()`, and if `CONFIG_DEBUG_VIRTUAL` is enabled, this translates to a call to `__phys_addr()`, which is in `physaddr.c`. This is bad, because KCOV is active during that call.

The first, simple fix would be to pre-evaluate `virt_to_phys(control_page)` *before* `load_segments()` and pass the result as an argument.  That would solve the issue, and avoid needing to disable KCOV in `physaddr.c`. But what about other code paths?

Now, let's consider `machine_kexec_64.o`. If KCOV is enabled for the `.o` file, the compiler will insert instrumentation throughout the function `machine_kexec()` itself.  That’s a problem. But wait, is `machine_kexec()` even *excluded* from KCOV? It isn't marked as `noinstr` or `__no_sanitize_coverage`. So, after `load_segments()`, there are more basic blocks in the function, where KCOV could trigger. This is particularly relevant in the conditional `if (image->preserve_context)` and the call to `relocate_kernel_ptr`. So it looks like `machine_kexec_64.o` needs to be excluded from KCOV, *or* parts of `machine_kexec()` and anything called after `load_segments()` should be marked as `noinstr` or `__no_kcov_instrument` (though the latter doesn't directly exist).

Thinking more broadly, there's another point... the function pointer `relocate_kernel_ptr`. The compiler could insert KCOV hooks *before* the function call. That's a concern. Even more critically, the `restore_processor_state()` call which happens afterwards.

Now, let's focus on `CONFIG_KEXEC_JUMP`. If `image->preserve_context` is true, the call to `relocate_kernel_ptr` *returns*. Where does it return? Back to `machine_kexec()`. Then we have:
```c
#ifdef CONFIG_KEXEC_JUMP
	if (image->preserve_context)
		restore_processor_state();
#endif

	__ftrace_enabled_restore(save_ftrace_enabled);
```
So we call `restore_processor_state()` which could touch per-cpu data. Now, the key question is what `relocate_kernel` in `arch/x86/kernel/relocate_kernel_64.S` actually *does*. Does it restore `GS` base *before* returning? I have to verify that.

Okay, here's what I've got after dissecting this KCOV-related patch. It looks like the author was aiming to fix some crashes during kexec, but the fix is incomplete and, frankly, a bit clumsy.

First, the author zeroed in on the crash that happens when KCOV is enabled and `kexec -e` is used (where the kernel is *not* returning). They saw it crash in `physaddr.c` and `machine_kexec_64.c` after `load_segments()`, which sets the `GS` register to zero. The core problem? They were calling `virt_to_phys(control_page)` *after* `load_segments()`, which, with `CONFIG_DEBUG_VIRTUAL` enabled, resolves to a call to `__phys_addr()` in `physaddr.c`.  Since `__phys_addr` is called with invalid `GS`, KCOV triggers a crash when trying to instrument the function call.  Their fix? Disable KCOV for `physaddr.c` and `machine_kexec_64.c`.  Problem solved, right? Not entirely.

Here's where the problem gets interesting. The author missed a *huge* edge case: what happens when `kexec -j` is used? When a return from `relocate_kernel_ptr()` happens, the CPU tries to execute the next basic block. `relocate_kernel_ptr` is called from `image->start = relocate_kernel_ptr(..., ...)` which is a function call. KCOV puts instrumentation around the beginning of basic blocks, which start at function calls, branches, and returns. If `image->preserve_context` is true, a basic block boundary is triggered. At this stage, `GS` is *still* zero, so the KCOV instrumentation in `restore_processor_state()` (in `cpu.c`) crashes the kernel when trying to read per-cpu variables via `GS`. They didn't test this scenario at all! They were only testing `kexec -e`.

The author could fix this cleanly if they restored the `GS` base within `relocate_kernel_64.S`. Then *none* of the instrumentation would crash. Right now, it doesn't.

Now, about that global KCOV disable for `physaddr.c`. This is my second concern. The comment explicitly says "Don't make any function call after here". Pre-calculating `virt_to_phys(control_page)` into a local variable before `load_segments()` completely eliminates the function call *after* `load_segments()`. This is clean, local, and addresses the real problem. Disabling KCOV for all of `physaddr.c` is overkill.  It results in lost coverage for vital memory primitives and hides potential bugs in this critical code. It's a valid code quality issue. We are losing coverage for functions like `__phys_addr` and it is completely unnecessary.

In short, there are two issues. One is a high-severity bug: the fix misses the `CONFIG_KEXEC_JUMP` path and leads to a crash when returning due to an invalid `GS` in `restore_processor_state()`. The other is a medium-severity problem: disabling KCOV for `physaddr.c` to "fix" `kexec -e` is an overly broad and unnecessarily heavy-handed approach. It's masking the underlying problem (the late function call) and causing coverage loss.

Author: Aleksandr Nogikh <nogikh@google.com>
x86/kexec: Disable KCOV instrumentation after load_segments()

This commit disables KCOV instrumentation for machine_kexec_64.o and
physaddr.o to prevent crashes during kexec. The crashes occur because
load_segments() invalidates the GS base, which KCOV relies on for per-CPU
data access.

> diff --git a/arch/x86/kernel/Makefile b/arch/x86/kernel/Makefile
> index e9aeeeafad17..41b1333907de 100644
> --- a/arch/x86/kernel/Makefile
> +++ b/arch/x86/kernel/Makefile
[ ... ]
> @@ -43,6 +43,16 @@ KCOV_INSTRUMENT_dumpstack_$(BITS).o			:= n
>  KCOV_INSTRUMENT_unwind_orc.o				:= n
>  KCOV_INSTRUMENT_unwind_frame.o				:= n
>  KCOV_INSTRUMENT_unwind_guess.o				:= n
> +# Disable KCOV to prevent crashes during kexec: load_segments() invalidates
> +# the GS base, which KCOV relies on for per-CPU data.
> +# As KCOV && KEXEC compatibility should be preserved (e.g. syzkaller is
> +# using it to collect crash dumps during kernel fuzzing), we could either
> +# selectively disable KCOV instrumentation, which can be fragile, or add
> +# more checks to KCOV, which would slow it down.
> +# As a compromise solution, let's disable KCOV instrumentation for the
> +# whole file. If its coverage is ever needed, we should consider other
> +# approaches.
> +KCOV_INSTRUMENT_machine_kexec_64.o			:= n

Does this fix cover the CONFIG_KEXEC_JUMP path where execution returns to a
KCOV-instrumented kernel?

During a kexec jump, relocate_kernel returns execution back to machine_kexec()
via the virtual_mapped label without restoring MSR_GS_BASE. The C code then
checks image->preserve_context and calls restore_processor_state() in
arch/x86/power/cpu.c.

Since arch/x86/power/cpu.o is not excluded from KCOV instrumentation, wouldn't
the compiler insert a call to __sanitizer_cov_trace_pc() at the entry of
restore_processor_state(), crashing the kernel when it attempts to access
per-CPU data via the invalid GS base?

> diff --git a/arch/x86/mm/Makefile b/arch/x86/mm/Makefile
> index 5b9908f13dcf..ea3a31b54e49 100644
> --- a/arch/x86/mm/Makefile
> +++ b/arch/x86/mm/Makefile
[ ... ]
> @@ -4,6 +4,16 @@ KCOV_INSTRUMENT_tlb.o			:= n
>  KCOV_INSTRUMENT_mem_encrypt.o		:= n
>  KCOV_INSTRUMENT_mem_encrypt_amd.o	:= n
>  KCOV_INSTRUMENT_pgprot.o		:= n
> +# Disable KCOV to prevent crashes during kexec: load_segments() invalidates
> +# the GS base, which KCOV relies on for per-CPU data.
> +# As KCOV && KEXEC compatibility should be preserved (e.g. syzkaller is
> +# using it to collect crash dumps during kernel fuzzing), we could either
> +# selectively disable KCOV instrumentation, which can be fragile, or add
> +# more checks to KCOV, which would slow it down.
> +# As a compromise solution, let's disable KCOV instrumentation for the
> +# whole file. If its coverage is ever needed, we should consider other
> +# approaches.
> +KCOV_INSTRUMENT_physaddr.o		:= n

Is disabling KCOV for all of physaddr.c an overly broad fix that causes
unnecessary loss of coverage for core memory primitives like __phys_addr()?

The crash in physaddr.c appears to happen because virt_to_phys(control_page)
is evaluated as an argument to relocate_kernel_ptr() after load_segments()
invalidates the GS base. Under CONFIG_DEBUG_VIRTUAL=y, virt_to_phys() expands
to an out-of-line call to __phys_addr().

This seems to violate the explicit comment in the code:
"load_segments() resets GS to 0. Don't make any function call after here".

Could we instead pre-evaluate virt_to_phys(control_page) into a local variable
before calling load_segments()? This would ensure no function calls are made
while the GS base is invalid, eliminating the need to disable KCOV for
physaddr.c entirely.

",

the compiler insert a call to __sanitizer_cov_trace_pc() at the entry of

> +KCOV_INSTRUMENT_physaddr.o		:= n

Is disabling KCOV for all of physaddr.c an overly broad fix that causes
unnecessary loss of coverage for core memory primitives like __phys_addr()?

The crash in physaddr.c appears to happen because virt_to_phys(control_page)
is evaluated as an argument to relocate_kernel_ptr() after load_segments()
invalidates the GS base. Under CONFIG_DEBUG_VIRTUAL=y, virt_to_phys() expands
to an out-of-line call to __phys_addr().

This seems to violate the explicit comment in the code:
"load_segments() resets GS to 0. Don't make any function call after here".

Could we instead pre-evaluate virt_to_phys(control_page) into a local variable
before calling load_segments()? This would ensure no function calls are made
while the GS base is invalid, eliminating the need to disable KCOV for
physaddr.c entirely.
